data.title: Solventum gains after activist Trian sends letter urging changes (update)
data.description:
Update 3:45pm: Adds details from Trian letter.

3M spinoff Solventum (SOLV) quickly rose 1.7% after activist investor Trian sent a new letter to push for changes at the company.

Nelson Peltz's Trian, which has a ~5% stake in Solventum (SOLV), is urging the company to separate its dental products and software operation.

"We have given the Company plenty of time to announce what we believe are obvious value creation initiatives, but our patience has run out," Trian wrote in a letter to the board. "If we do not see material improvement, we believe far greater changes will be necessary. We believe there is substantial value which can be realized at Solventum through responsible management ..."

Peltz's Trian is also taking issue with Solventum's (SOLV) capital allocation plans. Shares of Solventum have dropped 17% this year and are unchanged over the past year.

"We believe the Company has not been transparent about ways it can create value," Trian wrote in the letter. "Waiting to execute on portfolio and capital allocation opportunities has likely cost shareholders billions in additional value."

CNBC earlier reported on the Trian letter. 

Trian in January 2025 sent a letter to Solventum's (SOLV) board, urging the company to release a plan to restore its performance back  to levels it had inside of 3M (MMM). 

Bloomberg in July 2024 first reported that Trian had a stake and reached out to Solventum (SOLV) and wanted the company to reinvigorate organic sales, fix its margins, and evaluate asset sales.

data.title: Cvr partners expects Q2 2026 ammonia utilization at 95%-100% while projecting $60M-$75M in 2026 capex
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CVR Partners (UAN) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "The summarized financial highlights for the first quarter of 2026 include net sales of $180 million, net income of $50 million, EBITDA of $78 million and the Board of Directors declared a first quarter distribution of $4 per common unit" (CEO, President & Director Mark Pytosh).

  • "For the first quarter of 2026, our ammonia plant utilization was 103% with both plants running well and experiencing minimal downtime during the quarter" and "we also saw an increase in ammonia sales volume relative to the prior year period, along with increased sales prices for UAN and ammonia" (CEO Pytosh).

  • "The tightness in the nitrogen fertilizer market that began in the second half of 2025 has only been amplified by the conflicts in the Middle East over the past 2 months and leading to higher prices for the spring" (CEO Pytosh).

  • "We reported net income of $50 million or $4.72 per common unit" and "EBITDA was $78 million" (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Assistant Secretary Dane Neumann).

  • "We now believe we can achieve the feedstock diversification and capacity expansion of this project without investing the capital to source hydrogen from the adjacent Coffeyville refinery, which should significantly reduce the total capital spend associated with that scope of the project" (CEO Pytosh).

Outlook

  • "We estimate total capital spending for 2026 to be approximately $60 million to $75 million, of which $35 million to $45 million is expected to be maintenance capital" (CFO Neumann).

  • "Looking ahead to the second quarter of 2026, we estimate our ammonia utilization rate to be between 95% and 100%" and "direct operating expenses, excluding inventory and turnaround impacts, to be between $57 million and $62 million" (CFO Neumann).

  • "Total capital spending" for Q2 2026 is expected "to be between $28 million and $32 million" (CFO Neumann).

  • "I would expect" the Board "to continue to elect to reserve some capital, and we anticipate holding higher levels of cash related to these projects in the near term as we ramp up execution and spending" (CEO Pytosh).

Financial results

  • "For the first quarter of 2026, we reported net sales of $180 million and operating income of $58 million" (CFO Neumann).

  • "Total ammonia production for the first quarter was 220,000 gross tons, of which 70,000 net tons were available for sale and UAN production was 335,000 tons" (CFO Neumann).

  • "During the quarter, we sold approximately 310,000 tons of UAN at an average price of $343 per ton and approximately 73,000 tons of ammonia at an average price of $687 per ton" (CFO Neumann).

  • "Direct operating expenses for the first quarter of 2026 were $63 million" and "capital spending for the first quarter was $14 million, of which $8 million was maintenance capital" (CFO Neumann).

  • "We ended the quarter with total liquidity of $178 million, which consisted of $128 million in cash and availability under the ABL facility of $50 million" and "there was $42 million of cash available for distribution" (CFO Neumann).

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript: "At this time, there are no questions" (Operator).

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative (analysts) is not observable in the current quarter because "there are no questions" (Operator).

  • Slightly positive (management): "we had another strong quarter of operations" and "we will continue to focus on safely and reliably running our plants at high utilization levels" (CEO Pytosh).

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management’s tone shifted from addressing operational disappointment ("we were disappointed about the extended downtime") to emphasizing strong execution ("both plants running well") (CEO Pytosh).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Operations and distribution shifted materially from Q4 2025 to Q1 2026: "For the fourth quarter of 2025, our ammonia plant utilization was 64%" and the Board "declared a fourth quarter distribution of $0.37 per common unit" versus Q1 2026 "ammonia plant utilization was 103%" and a "distribution of $4 per common unit" (CEO, President & Director Mark Pytosh).

  • Q4 2025 was framed around downtime and turnaround impacts ("planned turnaround and subsequent delayed start-up") while Q1 2026 emphasized tight markets and geopolitical supply disruption ("conflicts in the Middle East have caused further disruptions to global supply") (CEO Pytosh).

  • Analyst focus changed from import flows, deferred revenue timing, and air separation reliability in Q4 ("what are you seeing in terms of UAN imports" and "do you feel confident about the air separator issue") to no questions in Q1 (Operator; Rob McGuire, Granite Research, LLC).

Risks and concerns

  • "Roughly 30% of nitrogen fertilizer production typically transiting through the Strait of Hormuz" was cited as a supply-chain exposure tied to the Middle East conflicts (CEO Pytosh).

  • "Multiple nitrogen fertilizer production facilities across the Middle East have been damaged or have curtailed production" and management added, "it remains unclear how long these issues in the Middle East and Russia will persist" (CEO Pytosh).

  • Cost pressure was flagged: "direct operating expenses increased... primarily due to higher natural gas and electricity costs, and repair and maintenance expenses" (CFO Neumann).

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a high-utilization quarter with $180 million in net sales, $78 million of EBITDA, and a $4 per-unit distribution, while emphasizing that recent Middle East conflicts and related natural gas dynamics have tightened global nitrogen supply. Executives also tied near-term cash reserves and higher liquidity to planned 2026 spending and reliability/capacity projects, including Coffeyville feedstock diversification and debottlenecking, alongside Q2 expectations for 95%-100% ammonia utilization, $57 million-$62 million in direct operating expenses (excluding certain impacts), and $28 million-$32 million in total capital spending.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Udr anticipates Q2 FFOA per share of $0.62 to $0.64 as it shifts to a monthly dividend
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UDR, Inc. (UDR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "2026 is off to a solid start" and "our first quarter results were in line with the expectations we provided at the beginning of the year," driven by "strong execution across operations and capital allocation," according to (Chairman, President & CEO Tom Toomey).

  • (Chairman, President & CEO Toomey) said UDR is "focused on taking advantage of the rare and likely fleeting opportunity to arbitrage a sizable gap in public and private market valuations," adding that a "data-driven and collaborative process led us to the decision to sell 4 assets" and that proceeds were used "to repurchase our shares and acquire an asset we gained access to through our debt and preferred equity program."

  • (Chairman, President & CEO Toomey) highlighted a shareholder-distribution change: "yesterday, we announced the transition to a monthly dividend" and "UDR is the first residential REIT to do so," while also stating, "we are maintaining our full year 2026 same-store and earnings guidance, which we will reassess next quarter."

  • (Senior VP & COO Michael Lacy) reported operating drivers that "came in as expected" including "blended lease rate growth of 1.6%, occupancy in the mid-96% range and mid-single-digit innovation income growth," and said "resident retention" was "300 basis points higher than the prior year," enabling "renewal rate growth of 5.2%."

  • (Senior VP & CFO David Bragg) said "first quarter FFO as adjusted per share of $0.62 achieved the midpoint of our guidance range" and provided Q2 guidance: "our second quarter FFOA per share guidance range is $0.62 to $0.64."

Outlook

  • (Senior VP & CFO Bragg) guided, "our second quarter FFOA per share guidance range is $0.62 to $0.64," and said the midpoint "represents an approximately 2% sequential increase" driven by "higher sequential NOI and accretion from share repurchases funded by dispositions."

  • (Chairman, President & CEO Toomey) said, "we have only completed the first 4 months of the year" and therefore, "we are maintaining our full year 2026 same-store and earnings guidance, which we will reassess next quarter."

  • (Senior VP & COO Lacy) reiterated the operating setup embedded in full-year assumptions: "our full year 2026 guidance assumes first half blended lease rate growth will be the same as the second half at 1.5% to 2%."

Financial Results

  • (Senior VP & CFO Bragg) stated, "first quarter FFO as adjusted per share of $0.62 achieved the midpoint of our guidance range," and said the "$0.02 sequential FFOA per share decline versus the fourth quarter of 2025" was driven by "a $0.03 decrease in NOI" from "higher sequential expenses" and "unusual weather," partially offset by "a $0.01 benefit from lower corporate expenses and G&A."

  • (Senior VP & COO Lacy) said "year-over-year same-store revenue growth" was "positive 90 basis points" and that "same-store expense growth of 4.4%" was elevated by winter storms; he added, "If normalizing for the approximately $1.4 million of incremental expenses," expense growth "would have been approximately 100 basis points better."

  • (Senior VP & CFO Bragg) detailed capital allocation activity: "we completed the sales of 4 apartment communities... for gross proceeds of $362 million," "received proceeds of approximately $139 million" from repayments, and "repurchased $150 million of shares, bringing total repurchase activity since September to $268 million."

Q&A

  • Eric Wolfe, Citigroup: asked if UDR would drive occupancy higher later in the year; (Senior VP & COO Lacy) replied, "we let occupancy come down in the second and third quarter" and later "may inch it up just a little bit, but maybe 10 or 20 bps."

  • James Feldman, Wells Fargo: asked about April trends and market acceleration/softness; (Senior VP & COO Lacy) said coastal blends were "about 3.1%" in April and Sunbelt trends "retreat slightly" to "negative 2.5% in April," while renewals were "between 5% to 5.5%" through July.

  • Steve Sakwa, Evercore ISI: asked about the debt and preferred equity book; (Senior VP & CFO Bragg) said to model "$300 million or so at the end of the year" from "the high $300 million range" at quarter end.

  • John Kim, BMO: challenged the monthly dividend as "a bit of a gimmick"; (Chairman, President & CEO Toomey) answered, "I'm really excited about the monthly dividend" and framed it as "shareholder of the future expansion opportunity," while (Senior VP & CFO Bragg) added it is "part of a broader push on our behalf to appeal to retail shareholders."

  • Michael Stefany, Mizuho (for Haendel St. Juste): asked about Massachusetts rent control risk and advocacy spending; (Senior VP of Investment Strategy Christopher Van Ens) said, "I don't think we're ready to handicap the risk yet" and noted contributions "in the neighborhood right now of around $0.5 million" that "most likely... will go higher."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts sentiment was slightly negative to skeptical on regulatory and demand risks, with questions probing "rent control" and whether federal actions were "real concerns" versus "a lot of noise" (Michael Stefany, Mizuho; Omotayo Okusanya, Deutsche Bank), alongside skepticism on the dividend strategy as "a bit of a gimmick" (John Kim, BMO).

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive and confident on operations and capital allocation, with (Chairman, President & CEO Toomey) stating "2026 is off to a solid start" and (Senior VP & COO Lacy) saying, "we are incredibly happy with the start to the year," while showing restraint on uncertain areas such as regulation: "I won't comment. I can't speculate" (Chairman, President & CEO Toomey) and "it's honestly too early" (Senior VP of Investment Strategy Van Ens).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on AI-enabled execution and 2026 setup, the current call carried more investor-base messaging and defensive posture on policy, including "we're actively engaged" to oppose Massachusetts (Senior VP of Investment Strategy Van Ens) alongside expanded discussion of the new "monthly dividend" (Chairman, President & CEO Toomey).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current call introduced a new shareholder strategy: "transition to a monthly dividend" and "first residential REIT" to do so (Chairman, President & CEO Toomey), while the prior call emphasized "ongoing investments in innovation" and "piloting AI initiatives" (Senior VP & COO Lacy).

  • Capital allocation execution advanced from intent to completed activity: previously UDR said, "we plan to be a net seller of assets in 2026" and was "actively marketing for sale numerous apartment communities" (Senior VP & CFO Bragg); in Q1, UDR reported it "completed the sales of 4 apartment communities" for "$362 million" and repurchased "$150 million" of stock (Senior VP & CFO Bragg).

  • Operating tone stayed constructive, shifting from early-year acceleration expectations ("first quarter blended lease rate growth should be between 1.5% and 2%" in Q4, per Senior VP & COO Lacy) to confirmation in Q1 that "blended lease rate growth of 1.6%" and occupancy "came in as expected" (Senior VP & COO Lacy).

Risks and Concerns

  • (Senior VP & COO Lacy) cited weather-related cost pressure: "same-store expense growth of 4.4% was elevated due to the impact of winter storms," including "approximately $1.4 million of incremental expenses."

  • (Senior VP of Investment Strategy Van Ens) flagged regulatory uncertainty as a direct risk to certain geographies, noting focus on "Massachusetts" and other areas including "Salinas, California," "New York City" and "D.C. proper," adding that such measures "would have a tangible direct impact potentially to our assets in those areas."

Final Takeaway

UDR management characterized Q1 as execution in-line with expectations, pairing operating momentum—"blended lease rate growth of 1.6%" and "renewal rate growth of 5.2%"—with an accelerated capital allocation playbook anchored in "public versus private market arbitrage" via dispositions, share repurchases, and selective acquisitions. Leadership also positioned the newly announced monthly dividend as a deliberate effort to broaden the shareholder base, while keeping full-year guidance unchanged and highlighting regulatory uncertainty—especially Massachusetts—as an active, but still early-stage, risk focus.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: T. Rowe Price signals 2026 adjusted operating expense growth of 3% to 6% while preparing mid-2026 launches
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: T. Rowe Price (TROW) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "While we continue to face outflows in our equity and mutual fund businesses, our teams are making progress in stabilizing flows and are advancing innovative strategies, new vehicles and compelling solutions to meet the needs of our clients." (President, CEO & Chair of the Board Robert Sharps)
  • "Our collaboration with Goldman Sachs is progressing with momentum building in model portfolios and product development advancing for the launch of an interval fund and target date sister series later this year." (President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps)
  • "Our ETFs generated over $2.8 billion in net flows in the first quarter. As of last week, our ETF assets under management surpassed $25 billion." (President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps)
  • "Our SMA platform expanded to 42 offerings with more than $17 billion in AUM and over $900 million in net flows in the first quarter." (President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps)
  • "Our adjusted earnings per share of $2.52 for Q1 2026 is up 3% from Q4 2025, and up 13% from Q1 2025." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jen Dardis)
  • "As previously reported, we ended the quarter with $1.71 trillion in AUM, and $13.7 billion in net outflows." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis)
  • "In aggregate, we currently have over $30 billion in dry powder across our various strategies." (Founder, VP & Director Glenn August)

Outlook

  • "We continue to expect 2026 adjusted operating expenses, excluding carried interest expense, to be up 3% to 6% over 2025's $4.6 billion." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis)
  • "While it's still too early to narrow our guidance, our expense forecast, which includes our investment in strategic priorities and market-driven expenses, remains comfortably within this range even with the market volatility experienced year-to-date." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis)
  • Compared with Q4 2025, management maintained the same expense growth range: "we anticipate 2026 adjusted operating expenses, excluding carried interest expense, will be up 3% to 6% over 2025's $4.6 billion." (CFO & Treasurer Dardis, Q4 2025)
  • Analysts’ estimates were provided, but the format did not match the required fiscalQuarter validation (Q1–Q4), so no estimates comparison is included.

Financial Results

  • "Our Q1 adjusted net revenue of over $1.8 billion was up 5% from Q1 2025, driven by higher investment advisory fees and accrued carried interest." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis)
  • "Investment advisory revenue for the quarter was almost $1.7 billion, up 5.3% from Q1 2025, and down 3.2% from Q4 2025." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis)
  • "Our Q1 annualized effective fee rate, excluding performance-based fees of 38.4 basis points is down from Q4 2025." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis)
  • "Our Q1 adjusted operating expenses, excluding accrued carried interest, were $1.14 billion" and were "a 7% decrease from Q4 2025." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis)
  • "Returning capital to our stockholders continues to be a priority, highlighted by our 40th consecutive annual increase in the quarterly dividend to $1.30 per share" and "purchasing $340 million worth of stock" in Q1. (VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis)

Q&A

  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies LLC: asked about wider-spread deployment and OHA’s software/AI exposure; Founder, VP & Director August said new-deal spread widening is "probably in the neighborhood of 25 to 50 basis points" and added, "We've avoided ARR loans." (Founder, VP & Director August)
  • Kenneth Worthington, JPMorgan: asked about normalization of spreads and client preparedness; Founder, VP & Director August responded that spreads are "in line with historic averages" and said investors see "300 to 400 basis points in the liquid credit market or 500 basis points in the private credit market" as attractive. (Founder, VP & Director August)
  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: asked whether ETF growth is new clients vs mutual fund migration and next steps (including Europe); President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps said, "we're both reaching new clients and serving existing clients" and added, "We are also developing plans to launch our first ETFs in Europe." (President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps)
  • Glenn Schorr, Evercore ISI: asked about April trends and institutional pipeline reallocations; President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps said the pipeline is "more as the latter" (slow-moving), while CIO Eric Veiel added, "we did see a trend towards non-U.S. assets" that "has picked back up again." (Head of Global Investments & Chief Investment Officer Veiel)
  • Alexander Bond, KBW: asked about wealth-channel product pipeline amid industry redemption pressure; Founder, VP & Director August pointed to OFLEX as "a new multi-strategy credit interval fund" and said they are "in development" on additional products. (Founder, VP & Director August)
  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: pressed on expenses after a softer Q1; VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis said Q1 is typically softer because "our year-end compensation is struck in Q4" and cited "realignment within our marketing teams" and "rationalization of our real estate footprint" alongside continued strategic investment. (VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis)
  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: requested software allocation disclosure and AI underwriting process; Founder, VP & Director August said allocation is "in the neighborhood of 15% to 20%" and added, "we have AI risk management -- risk management tools in terms of rating each one of our companies." (Founder, VP & Director August)
  • Patrick Davitt, Autonomous Research: asked about appetite to use balance sheet for alternatives M&A; President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps said, "we'll participate in that consolidation over time" if there is "cultural fit" and later added, "We're going to be really judicious with regard to ultimately how we deploy" cash. (President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on private-credit resilience and transparency, with repeated probes on AI disruption and software exposure, including "it would be great to get that number." (Brennan Hawken, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research)
  • Management’s prepared remarks were slightly positive on product momentum and partnerships, while Q&A responses were more explanatory and process-focused, including "we're going to invest in our ability to support our clients" (President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps) and "we continuously re-underwrite" (Founder, VP & Director August).
  • Versus Q4 2025, the current quarter featured more risk-oriented questioning tied to "AI disruption" and market volatility, while the prior call leaned more heavily into target-date share shifts and operating leverage questions (e.g., "overall margins remain relatively healthy, but below where you guys have been in the past"). (Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs, Q4 2025)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized ETFs/SMA scaling, alternatives (OHA) positioning, and execution milestones such as "We closed our first T. Rowe Price-managed CLO in early April" and "preparations underway" for a "targeted mid-2026 launch" with First Abu Dhabi Bank. (President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps)
  • Q4 2025 centered on year-end flow dynamics and target-date category share shifts, with management stating, "fully active Target Date funds are losing share to passive and blend" while positioning blend/hybrid as the offset. (President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps, Q4 2025)
  • Management kept 2026 expense guidance unchanged quarter to quarter at "up 3% to 6%" over 2025. (VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis)

Risks and Concerns

  • "We continue to face outflows in our equity and mutual fund businesses" alongside fee-rate pressure, with the CFO noting mix shifts are "compressing effective fee rate." (President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps; VP, CFO & Treasurer Dardis)
  • Private-credit headlines and wealth-channel liquidity pressure were addressed as an industry issue: "Requests for liquidity across non-traded BDCs... have increased meaningfully across the industry" and sometimes exceed "the 5% quarterly limit." (Founder, VP & Director August)
  • Geopolitical and macro uncertainty was framed as a driver of volatility: "markets declined in March in response to the conflict with Iran" and the war "has disrupted global trade" and "caused a massive spike in energy prices." (President, CEO & Chair of the Board Sharps; Founder, VP & Director August)

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a quarter of continued equity/mutual-fund outflows and fee-rate compression alongside scaling growth vectors, highlighting ETF and SMA inflows, ongoing target-date blend momentum, and an expanded alternatives narrative led by OHA. The company reiterated its 2026 expense growth range of 3% to 6% (excluding carried interest), pointed to capital returns through a $1.30 quarterly dividend and $340 million of buybacks in Q1, and addressed investor focus on AI disruption and private-credit positioning by emphasizing underwriting discipline, software exposure context, and product development across wealth, retirement, and insurance channels.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Aflac targets Japan sales closer to JPY 80B while expanding reinsurance franchise in Japan
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Aflac Incorporated (AFL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “The first quarter marked a good start to the year. Aflac Incorporated reported net earnings per diluted share of $1.98 and adjusted earnings per diluted share of $1.75.” (Chairman & CEO Daniel Amos)
  • “I am pleased with Aflac Japan's sales increase of a 25.5% increase for the first quarter.” (Chairman & CEO Daniel Amos) He said results were “driven largely by our newest medical product, Anshin Palette and Miraito, our latest cancer insurance product,” while the company continues to promote “Tsumitasu” to “new and younger customers.” (Chairman & CEO Amos)
  • “Turning to Aflac U.S., I am encouraged by the 2.9% year-over-year increase in sales,” and “we maintained strong premium persistency of 79.3% and increased net earned premium of 3.5% for the quarter.” (Chairman & CEO Amos)
  • “Combining share repurchase and dividends, we delivered $1.3 billion back to the shareholders in the first quarter.” (Chairman & CEO Daniel Amos)
  • “For the first quarter of 2026, adjusted earnings per diluted share increased 6.6% year-over-year to $1.77, excluding effect of foreign currency in the quarter.” (Senior EVP & CFO Max Broden)

Outlook

  • “We still feel very confident with the outlook range that we gave at the beginning of the year of 60% to 63% for the Japan benefit ratio.” (Senior EVP & CFO Max Broden)
  • “The benefit ratio guidance for the full year remains 48% to 52%.” (Senior EVP & CFO Max Broden)
  • On Japan earned premium trajectory, management reiterated a near-term decline expectation: “For the time being, we have been sort of hovering in this range of negative 1% to 2% on the underlying earned premium, and that's what we expect for the full year.” (Senior EVP & CFO Broden)
  • On Japan sales ambition for 2026, “I think the number will be higher than last year's number,” and “I would say closer to JPY 80 billion. That's what I'd like.” (Chairman & CEO Daniel Amos)

Financial Results

  • Earnings metrics were framed in two ways: “net earnings per diluted share of $1.98 and adjusted earnings per diluted share of $1.75” (Chairman & CEO Daniel Amos), and “adjusted earnings per diluted share increased 6.6% year-over-year to $1.77, excluding effect of foreign currency” (Senior EVP & CFO Max Broden).
  • Japan profitability and drivers: “Japan's total benefit ratio came in at 62.9% for the quarter,” “our expense ratio in Japan was 19.5%,” and “the pretax margin for Japan in the quarter was 35%, up 320 basis points year-over-year.” (Senior EVP & CFO Broden)
  • U.S. profitability and costs: “Net earned premiums were up 3.5%,” “our total benefit ratio came in at 47.2%,” “our expense ratio in the U.S. was 38.3%,” and “pretax margin of 20.4%.” (Senior EVP & CFO Broden)
  • Corporate & Other moved to “breakeven pretax adjusted earnings, down from a $43 million gain last year,” which Broden tied to “lower adjusted net investment income, higher interest expense and operating costs and runoff impacts from closed blocks of business.” (Senior EVP & CFO Broden)
  • Investment/credit items included “$19 million of charge-offs on our loan portfolio” and “$24 million of impairments on our real estate owned portfolio,” as Broden said valuations remained “depressed” in commercial real estate markets. (Senior EVP & CFO Broden)
  • Reinsurance expansion marker: “Effective March 31, Aflac Re Bermuda entered into a transaction, in which it assumed a block of whole life annuities from Japan Post Insurance,” and “this transaction itself is immaterial to Aflac Inc.'s financials, but it marks a strategic milestone as we expand our reinsurance franchise, targeting the Japan market.” (Senior EVP & CFO Max Broden)
  • Balance sheet and capital deployment: “Aflac Inc. unencumbered liquidity stood at $3.4 billion,” “our adjusted leverage was 21.2%,” “estimated regulatory ESR of 227%,” “ESR with USP of 243%,” “combined RBC to be approximately 560%,” and “we repurchased $1 billion of our own stock and paid dividends of $315 million in Q1.” (Senior EVP & CFO Broden)

Q&A

  • Thomas Gallagher, Evercore ISI: asked about reinsurance capital benefit and Japan ESR headwinds; Senior EVP & CFO Broden responded, “The impacts to capital were relatively small,” and said ESR movement was mainly “subsidiary dividends being moved up from Aflac Japan to the holding company,” with “higher yen rates” slightly negative and “a little bit of a yen weakening” benefiting ESR.
  • Thomas Gallagher, Evercore ISI: asked about lapse-and-reissue economics on younger customers; Senior EVP & CFO Broden said “relatively minor impacts on the IRRs” and “the overall impact to IRRs across the whole in-force is expected to be quite minor.”
  • Ryan Krueger, KBW: asked about Japan benefit ratio path toward outlook; Senior EVP & CFO Broden said, “We still feel very confident with the outlook range... of 60% to 63%,” citing experience and lapse mix considerations.
  • Ryan Krueger, KBW: asked how large Japan reinsurance could become; Senior EVP & CFO Broden said blocks “could be material to us over time” and “we certainly expect that this would be material to the company.”
  • Wesley Carmichael, Wells Fargo: asked about Miraito momentum; Senior Managing Exec. Officer Koichiro Yoshizumi said, “Miraito's momentum is continuing,” and “we expect the 2026 sales to be equivalent to the 2025,” while noting a structure to sell “Miraito, medical insurance Anshin Palette, and Tsumitasu... concurrently.”
  • Joel Hurwitz, Dowling: asked earnings impact from Japan external reinsurance; Senior EVP & CFO Broden said it “negatively impacted our Aflac Japan earnings in the first quarter by mid-single-digit U.S. dollars in millions,” and “over time, it will go towards more of a zero impact.”
  • Joel Hurwitz, Dowling: asked about state-driven U.S. rate cuts; President Virgil Miller said, “No, we're not seeing any additional pressure like that,” and “we're really seeing no material impacts at all.”
  • Suneet Kamath, Jefferies: questioned whether reinsurance signals weaker Japan core growth; Chairman & CEO Amos said, “No,” adding, “I like evolution, not revolution.”
  • Suneet Kamath, Jefferies: pressed on U.S. core agent business; President Miller said it was “slightly down to flat,” adding, “we're not seeing growth out of our core traditional business,” while citing “a 16% conversion rate of new agents.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to skeptical, pressing on Japan growth translation and U.S. core channel, including “your read” that reinsurance might imply less growth (Jefferies Kamath) and “why that's not translating to better earned premium growth” framing (BMO Matten).
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A; examples included “we still feel very confident with the outlook range” (Senior EVP & CFO Broden) and “No” to the reinsurance-as-growth-signal framing (Chairman & CEO Amos).
  • Versus last quarter, the analyst pushback shifted more toward earned premium inflection mechanics and capital deployment, while management again emphasized methodical strategy, with “evolution, not revolution” (Chairman & CEO Amos) echoing a more measured posture.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Japan sales acceleration was described at “25.5%” in Q1 2026 vs. “15.7% for the fourth quarter” of 2025 previously discussed by the CEO. (Chairman & CEO Daniel Amos)
  • Japan profitability commentary improved sequentially in prepared remarks, with “pretax margin for Japan in the quarter was 35%” vs. “31.3%” in Q4 2025. (Senior EVP & CFO Max Broden)
  • U.S. expense ratio improved to “38.3%” from “40.4%” in Q4 2025, while Corporate & Other swung to “breakeven” from a “(-$31 million)” pretax adjusted loss previously. (Senior EVP & CFO Broden)
  • Capital return increased to “$1 billion” of buybacks in Q1 2026 vs. “$800 million” in Q4 2025, while liquidity moved to “$3.4 billion” from “$4.1 billion.” (Senior EVP & CFO Broden)

Risks and Concerns

  • Japan in-force pressure remains central: “Net earned premiums in yen terms for the quarter declined 3.8%,” and Broden cited “an uptick in lapse and reissue on our cancer insurance product,” with persistency “down... at 92.8%.” (Senior EVP & CFO Max Broden)
  • Commercial real estate marks were reiterated: “$24 million of impairments on our real estate owned portfolio,” tied to “continued depressed valuations.” (Senior EVP & CFO Broden)
  • Corporate earnings volatility was flagged as rate-sensitive: “The main driver that is swinging our Corporate and Other segment around is the net investment income that we generate on our cash and liquid assets.” (Senior EVP & CFO Broden)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as a “good start to the year,” highlighting Japan sales growth driven by Anshin Palette and Miraito, stable-to-strong U.S. premium persistency, and elevated capital return in the quarter. Executives reiterated full-year benefit ratio ranges (Japan 60% to 63%; U.S. 48% to 52%), acknowledged continued pressure on Japan underlying earned premiums, and positioned the Japan Post reinsurance transaction as “immaterial” financially but a strategic step in building a Japan-focused external reinsurance franchise, while keeping buybacks and dividends central to capital deployment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lilly projects 2026 revenue of $82B-$85B and non-GAAP EPS of $35.50-$37 as Foundayo launch begins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "2026 is off to a strong start" and "earlier this month, we achieved an important milestone as orforglipron was approved by the U.S. FDA under the trade name Foundayo" (Chairman & CEO David Ricks). "Regulatory reviews are ongoing in over 40 countries" and "we plan to submit Foundayo in the U.S. for type 2 diabetes later this quarter" (Chairman & CEO Ricks).
  • "Revenue grew 56% compared to Q1 2025" and "we also distributed $1.5 billion in dividends in the first quarter and executed $2.4 billion in share repurchases" (Chairman & CEO Ricks).
  • "Gross margin as a percentage of revenue was 82.6%" and "non-GAAP earnings per share was $8.55, including acquired R&D charges of $0.52" (Executive VP & CFO Lucas Montarce). "We have increased the top and the bottom end of the revenue range by $2 billion" and "now expect non-GAAP earnings per share of $35.50 to $37" (Executive VP & CFO Montarce).
  • "Foundayo met the primary endpoint of non-inferiority with a 16% lower risk of MACE-4 events" and "in a preplanned analysis not controlled for multiplicity, the survival advantage for patients on Foundayo was 57% compared to insulin glargine" (Chief Scientific & Product Officer Daniel Skovronsky).

Outlook

  • "We have increased the top and the bottom end of the revenue range by $2 billion and now expect full year revenue to be between $82 billion and $85 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Montarce). "We still expect price to be a headwind in the low to mid-teens for the full year" (Executive VP & CFO Montarce).
  • "We expect our non-GAAP performance margin to be between 47% and 48.5%" and "anticipate regulatory action before the end of this year" for the U.S. type 2 diabetes filing tied to ACHIEVE-4 (Executive VP & CFO Montarce; Chief Scientific & Product Officer Skovronsky).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s 2026 framework of "revenue to be between $80 billion and $83 billion" and "earnings per share of between $33.50 and $35" (Executive VP & CFO Montarce, Q4 2025), Lilly lifted both the revenue and non-GAAP EPS ranges.

Financial Results

  • "Revenue grew 56% compared to Q1 2025, driven by Zepbound and Mounjaro" (Executive VP & CFO Montarce). "Marketing, selling and administrative expenses increased 19%" and "R&D expenses increased 28%" (Executive VP & CFO Montarce).
  • "Mounjaro and Zepbound global revenue was $12.8 billion combined" and "U.S. price declined by 7%" with the caveat that "excluding this impact, U.S. price will have declined 10%" (Executive VP & CFO Montarce).
  • "Self-pay continues to be an important segment for Zepbound and accounted for approximately 45% of total Zepbound prescriptions in Q1 and 55% of new prescriptions" and "the loss of Medicaid access in certain states had a negative impact on Q1 prescription growth in the high single digits" (Executive VP & CFO Montarce).

Q&A

  • Geoffrey Meacham, Citi: "can you talk about...the margins under a wide range of price scenarios"; Chairman & CEO Ricks: "pretty much every time we reduce pricing, we see a pretty large expansion" and "at the margin, we do have latitude."
  • Christopher Schott, JPMorgan: "should we expect any impact...from the entry of generic sema"; Executive VP & President of Lilly International Patrik Jonsson: "it seems like it's really stimulating the growth in the overall obesity market" and "Mounjaro has actually been holding market share quite nicely."
  • Asad Haider, Goldman Sachs: referenced Foundayo starts and asked about guidance contribution; Executive VP & President Lilly USA Ilya Yuffa: "we now have over 8,000 prescribers" and "we now have just over 20,000 patients treated to date"; Executive VP & CFO Montarce: "the increase on our guide is driven by the strength of the entire portfolio" and Foundayo is "tracking to our expectations."
  • Umer Raffat, Evercore ISI: asked about Zepbound net price math; Executive VP & CFO Montarce: "your math is pretty spot on" and pointed to mix ("commercial business") while Chairman & CEO Ricks added "medical exception and OSA usage...at close to an undiscounted price."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on pricing and channel mix, with questions centered on "pricing in incretins," "generic sema," and net price mechanics (Meacham, Schott, Raffat).
  • Management tone stayed confident in prepared remarks ("confident in our ability to deliver another year of industry-leading growth") and explanatory in Q&A, emphasizing elasticity ("volume...perhaps nonlinear to price reductions") and long-run investment flexibility (Chairman & CEO Ricks).
  • Versus Q4 2025’s forward-setup tone around a planned oral launch, Q1 2026 sentiment shifted to execution tracking with "early feedback...encouraging" and payor access specifics (Executive VP & CFO Montarce).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The key shift was from planning for an oral GLP-1 launch ("expect orforglipron to launch...during the second quarter of 2026," Q4 2025) to reporting an FDA approval and launch execution ("Foundayo...was broadly available in pharmacies on April 9") (Chairman & CEO Ricks; Executive VP & CFO Montarce).
  • Guidance language moved upward: Q4 2025 set "$80 billion to $83 billion" revenue and "$33.50 to $35" non-GAAP EPS; Q1 2026 raised to "$82 billion and $85 billion" revenue and "$35.50 to $37" non-GAAP EPS (Executive VP & CFO Montarce).

Risks and Concerns

  • Lilly reiterated pricing pressure: "We still expect price to be a headwind in the low to mid-teens" (Executive VP & CFO Montarce).
  • Access volatility remained a theme, with "loss of Medicaid access in certain states" affecting Q1 scripts and management describing Medicare Bridge timing as a ramp that "will take time to build" (Executive VP & CFO Montarce; Executive VP & President Lilly USA Yuffa).

Final Takeaway

Lilly described Q1 2026 as a launch-and-scale quarter, anchored by Foundayo’s FDA approval and early access buildout alongside continued demand for Mounjaro and Zepbound. Management raised 2026 revenue and non-GAAP EPS guidance ranges, while repeatedly flagging price as a headwind and highlighting volume expansion dynamics, evolving payer access (including the Medicare Bridge program timeline), and ongoing pipeline and business development activity as the operational focus for the remainder of 2026.
